steps.title: Identify the chronological order
steps.content: UK march: October 12 – November 16, 1912. US march: December 16 – December 28, 1912. The UK march clearly came first.

steps.title: Match the goal
steps.content: The student wants to emphasize the order in which the two marches occurred, so the correct sentence must explicitly sequence UK first, US second.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘After suffragists in the UK marched from Edinburgh to London, suffragists in the US marched from New York City to Albany, New York.’: uses "After" to place the UK march before the US march — correct chronological order. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘In the early 1900s, suffragists in the UK and the US marched for women's voting rights.’: groups the marches in a single time frame without showing which came first — doesn't accomplish the goal. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘A march from New York City to Albany, New York, was followed by one that began in Edinburgh and ended in London.’: reverses the actual order (puts the US march first), misrepresenting the dates. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘From October 12 to November 16, 1912, suffragists in the UK marched from Edinburgh to London.’: describes only one of the two marches, so it can't show their relative order. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
